result: I keep thinking about the way light looks when it pools on the kitchen floor at 3:17 p.m., a private gold that belongs only to the houseplants and the crumbs from lunch. It’s ordinary, but it feels like a secret I’m not supposed to notice, like the universe is whispering, “Here, have a little miracle on the house.” Time does that—hides wonder in plain sight, then charges interest when we finally notice. Yesterday I tried to hold the light in my palm like water, but it slipped through the cracks between my fingers and kept traveling across the room, up the wall, over the calendar still flipped to March even though the lilacs outside insist it’s May. Somewhere between the second hand and the humming refrigerator, I realized I’m always measuring life in units that don’t fit: loads of laundry, unread emails, paychecks cut into thirds before they even land. Yet the light doesn’t measure anything; it just arrives, reheats the dust motes, and leaves without asking to be remembered. I want to live like that—unclenched, unaccountable, a guest who forgets to take her shoes off and is welcomed anyway. So tonight I’ll leave the blinds open, let the dark come in too, let it mingle with the furniture. Maybe the moon will spill a softer version of the same secret across the table, and maybe I’ll finally believe that being alive is less about holding light and more about agreeing to glow inside it while it lasts.
